era_context: The year 761 CE falls during the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE) in China, the consolidation of the Abbasid Caliphate in the Islamic world, and the reign of Pepin III in the Frankish Kingdom. This was a period of significant military conflict and administrative reorganization across major Eurasian powers, with limited documentation for most of the world outside these centers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish realm

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion: Sixth year of conflict; Tang Dynasty under severe strain
events.description: The An Lushan Rebellion, which had begun in 755 CE, continued in its sixth year in 761 CE. By this time, the initial rebel leader An Lushan had died in 757 CE, and his successor Shi Siming was leading the rebellion. The conflict had devastated northern China, disrupted the Grand Canal trade route, and severely weakened the Tang Dynasty's military and fiscal capacity. The rebellion had killed an estimated 10–15 million people (though this figure is debated), making it one of the deadliest conflicts in human history. By 761, the Tang was in severe fiscal crisis, with military campaigns consuming vast resources. The rebellion would finally be suppressed in 763 CE.

sources.name: New Book of Tang (Xin Tang Shu), compiled by Ouyang Xiu et al., 11th century
Old Book of Tang (Jiu Tang Shu), compiled by Liu Xu et al., 10th century

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the An Lushan Rebellion in 761 CE is clearly documented in Chinese historical sources. The date is well-attested.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate: al-Mansur consolidates power; administrative reorganization
events.description: Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) continued the consolidation of Abbasid power in 761 CE. By this time, the Abbasid Revolution (750 CE) had been successful in overthrowing the Umayyad Caliphate, but consolidating control over the vast Islamic empire remained a challenge. Al-Mansur was engaged in suppressing remaining Umayyad loyalists, establishing centralized control, and reorganizing the administration along Persian bureaucratic models. The Barmakid family, of Persian origin, was rising in prominence as administrators. This period saw the shift from Arab tribal governance to a centralized, multi-ethnic state. In 762 CE, al-Mansur would found Baghdad as the new capital, symbolizing this transformation.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of al-Mansur's consolidation is well-attested in Islamic historiography, though specific events in 761 CE are not always precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Frankish Kingdom: Pepin III's military campaigns continue
events.region: Frankish Kingdom (modern France, Germany, Low Countries)
events.description: Pepin III (r. 751–768 CE) continued his military campaigns in the early 760s, consolidating Frankish power in Aquitaine and the Pyrenees. By 761, Pepin had established the Carolingian dynasty as the dominant military power in Western Europe. His campaigns against Aquitanian nobles and preparations for the succession of his sons, Charlemagne and Carloman, were ongoing. These military campaigns and administrative consolidations provided the foundation for Charlemagne's later expansion and the creation of the Carolingian Empire.
events.certainty_note: The Royal Frankish Annals provide near-contemporary documentation of Frankish military campaigns in the 760s, though specific events in 761 CE are not always detailed.

events.title: Byzantine Empire: Constantine V continues military campaigns
events.description: Emperor Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE) continued his military campaigns against Arab forces in the Mediterranean and against the Bulgars in the Balkans. Constantine V was one of the most militarily active Byzantine emperors, and his reign saw numerous campaigns to defend and expand Byzantine territory. By 761, he had established himself as a formidable military leader, though his reign was also marked by the Iconoclastic Controversy, which created internal religious and political tensions.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine sources document Constantine V's military activities in the 760s, but specific events in 761 CE are not always clearly dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The casualty figures for the An Lushan Rebellion (often cited as 10–15 million deaths) are debated by modern scholars. Some argue these figures are exaggerated in later sources, while others suggest they may be underestimated. Additionally, the narrative of the rebellion as a simple 'military revolt' obscures its complex origins in regional military autonomy, ethnic tensions, and fiscal crisis. The significance of the rebellion in marking the 'decline' of the Tang Dynasty is somewhat overstated; the dynasty continued for another 150 years and remained a major power, though its earlier dominance was diminished.
historiographic_note: Sources for 761 CE are concentrated in China and the Islamic world, where court historiography was well-developed. Chinese sources are relatively detailed for the An Lushan Rebellion, though they reflect court perspectives and may underrepresent popular experiences. Islamic sources are less detailed for specific events in 761 CE and are often compiled centuries after the fact. Byzantine sources are somewhat more contemporary but still subject to political bias. Western European sources are sparse and often legendary. The year 761 CE is significant primarily for events in East Asia, the Islamic world, and the Byzantine Empire; most of the rest of the world has no documented events for this year.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the An Lushan Rebellion that began in 755 CE
Continued fighting in 761 CE led to the rebellion's suppression in 763 CE
Abbasid consolidation followed the revolution of 750 CE
Al-Mansur's consolidation led to the founding of Baghdad in 762 CE
Pepin III's consolidation prepared the way for Charlemagne's succession
